subject: Physics
question_text: A light bulb is rated at 100W for a 220 V supply. Find the resistance of the bulb; the peak voltage of the source; and the rms current through the bulb.
question_html:
A light bulb is rated at 100W for a 220 V supply. Find

  1. the resistance of the bulb;
  2. the peak voltage of the source; and
  3. the rms current through the bulb.
solution_text: We are given P = 100 W and V = 220 V. The resistance of the bulb is {tex}R=\frac{V^{2}}{P}=\frac{(220 \mathrm{V})^{2}}{100 \mathrm{W}}=484 \Omega{/tex} The peak voltage of the source is {tex}v_{m}=\sqrt{2} V=311 \mathrm{V} {/tex} Since, P = lV thus rms cureent is given by, {tex}I=\frac{P}{V}=\frac{100 \mathrm{W}}{220 \mathrm{V}}{/tex} = 0.454 A
solution_html:
1. We are given P = 100 W and V = 220 V. The resistance of the bulb is 
    {tex}R=\frac{V^{2}}{P}=\frac{(220 \mathrm{V})^{2}}{100 \mathrm{W}}=484 \Omega{/tex}
  2. The peak voltage of the source is
    {tex}v_{m}=\sqrt{2} V=311 \mathrm{V} {/tex}
  3. Since, P = lV   thus rms cureent is given by,
    {tex}I=\frac{P}{V}=\frac{100 \mathrm{W}}{220 \mathrm{V}}{/tex} = 0.454 A

subject: Physics
question_text: A pure inductor of 25.0 mH is connected to a source of 220 V. Find the inductive reactance and rms current in the circuit if the frequency of the source is 50 Hz.
question_html: A pure inductor of 25.0 mH is connected to a source of 220 V. Find the inductive reactance and rms current in the circuit if the frequency of the source is 50 Hz.
solution_text: The inductive reactance, {tex}X_{L}=2 \pi v L=2 \times 3.14{/tex} {tex}\times 50 \times 25 \times 10^{-3} \Omega{/tex} = 7.85 {tex}\Omega{/tex} The rms current in the circuit is {tex}I=\frac{V}{X_{L}}=\frac{220 \mathrm{V}}{7.85 \Omega}=28 \mathrm{A}{/tex}
solution_html:
The inductive reactance,
{tex}X_{L}=2 \pi v L=2 \times 3.14{/tex} {tex}\times 50 \times 25 \times 10^{-3} \Omega{/tex}
= 7.85 {tex}\Omega{/tex}
The rms current in the circuit is
{tex}I=\frac{V}{X_{L}}=\frac{220 \mathrm{V}}{7.85 \Omega}=28 \mathrm{A}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_text: A lamp is connected in series with a capacitor. Predict your observations for dc and ac connections. What happens in each case if the capacitance of the capacitor is reduced?
question_html: A lamp is connected in series with a capacitor. Predict your observations for dc and ac connections. What happens in each case if the capacitance of the capacitor is reduced?
solution_text: When the dc source is connected, the condenser is charged but no current flows in the circuit. Therefore, the lamp does not glow. No change occurs even when the capacitance of the capacitor is reduced. When ac source is connected the capacitors offers capacitance reactance X C = {tex}\frac{1}{\omega C}{/tex} . The current flow in the circuit and the lamp glows. On reducing C; X C increases. Therefore, the glow of the bulb reduces.
solution_html:
When the dc source is connected, the condenser is charged but no current flows in the circuit. Therefore, the lamp does not glow. No change occurs even when the capacitance of the capacitor is reduced.
When ac source is connected the capacitors offers capacitance reactance XC = {tex}\frac{1}{\omega C}{/tex}. The current flow in the circuit and the lamp glows. On reducing C; XC increases. Therefore, the glow of the bulb reduces.

subject: Physics
question_text: A 15.0 {tex}\mu \mathrm{F}{/tex} capacitor is connected to a 220 V, 50 Hz source. Find the capacitive reactance and the current (rms and peak) in the circuit. If the frequency is doubled, what happens to the capacitive reactance and the current?
question_html: A 15.0 {tex}\mu \mathrm{F}{/tex} capacitor is connected to a 220 V, 50 Hz source. Find the capacitive reactance and the current (rms and peak) in the circuit. If the frequency is doubled, what happens to the capacitive reactance and the current?
solution_text: C = 15 {tex}\mu{/tex} F. and voltage = 220v. The capacitive reactance is {tex}X_{C}=\frac{1}{2 \pi v C}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{2 \pi(50 \mathrm{Hz})\left(15.0 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{F}\right)}=212 \Omega{/tex} The rms current is {tex}I=\frac{V}{X_{C}}=\frac{220 \mathrm{V}}{212 \Omega}=1.04 \mathrm{A}{/tex} The peak current is i m = {tex}\sqrt{2} I{/tex} = (1.41) (1.04 A) = 1.47 A This current oscillates between +1.47A and –1.47 A and is ahead of the voltage by {tex}\frac {\pi} {2}{/tex} . If the frequency is doubled, the capacitive reactance is halved and consequently, the current is doubled.
solution_html:
C = 15{tex}\mu{/tex}F. and voltage = 220v. 
The capacitive reactance is
{tex}X_{C}=\frac{1}{2 \pi v C}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{2 \pi(50 \mathrm{Hz})\left(15.0 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{F}\right)}=212 \Omega{/tex}
The rms current is
{tex}I=\frac{V}{X_{C}}=\frac{220 \mathrm{V}}{212 \Omega}=1.04 \mathrm{A}{/tex}
The peak current is
im = {tex}\sqrt{2} I{/tex} = (1.41) (1.04 A) = 1.47 A
This current oscillates between +1.47A and –1.47 A and is ahead of the voltage by {tex}\frac {\pi} {2}{/tex}.
If the frequency is doubled, the capacitive reactance is halved and consequently, the current is doubled.

subject: Physics
question_text: A light bulb and an open coil inductor are connected to an ac source through a key as shown in Fig. The switch is closed and after some time, an iron rod is inserted into the interior of the inductor. The glow of the light bulb increases; decreases; is unchanged, as the iron rod is inserted. Give your answer with reasons.
question_html:
A light bulb and an open coil inductor are connected to an ac source through a key as shown in Fig.

The switch is closed and after some time, an iron rod is inserted into the interior of the inductor. The glow of the light bulb

  1. increases;
  2. decreases;
  3. is unchanged, as the iron rod is inserted.

Give your answer with reasons.
solution_text: The glow of the bulb decreases, As the iron rod is inserted, the magnetic field inside the coil magnetizes the iron increasing the magnetic field inside it. Hence, the inductance of the coil increases. Consequently, the inductive reactance of the coil increases. As a result, a larger fraction of the applied ac voltage appears across the inductor, leaving less voltage across the bulb. Therefore, the glow of the light bulb decreases.
solution_html: The glow of the bulb decreases, As the iron rod is inserted, the magnetic field inside the coil magnetizes the iron increasing the magnetic field inside it. Hence, the inductance of the coil increases. Consequently, the inductive reactance of the coil increases. As a result, a larger fraction of the applied ac voltage appears across the inductor, leaving less voltage across the bulb. Therefore, the glow of the light bulb decreases.

subject: Physics
question_text: A resistor of 200 {tex}\Omega{/tex} and a capacitor of 15.0 {tex}\mu \mathrm{F}{/tex} are connected in series to a 220 V, 50 Hz ac source. Calculate the current in the circuit; Calculate the voltage (rms) across the resistor and the capacitor. Is the algebraic sum of these voltages more than the source voltage? If yes, resolve the paradox.
question_html:
A resistor of 200 {tex}\Omega{/tex} and a capacitor of 15.0 {tex}\mu \mathrm{F}{/tex} are connected in series to a 220 V, 50 Hz ac source.

  1. Calculate the current in the circuit;
  2. Calculate the voltage (rms) across the resistor and the capacitor. Is the algebraic sum of these voltages more than the source voltage? If yes, resolve the paradox.
solution_text: Given R = 200 {tex}\Omega{/tex} C = 15.0 {tex}\mu \mathrm{F}{/tex} = 15.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -6 F V = 220 V, {tex}\nu{/tex} = 50 Hz In order to calculate the current, we need the impedance of the circuit. It is {tex}Z=\sqrt{R^{2}+X_{C}^{2}}=\sqrt{R^{2}+(2 \pi \nu C)^{2}}{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{(200 \Omega)^{2}+\left(2 \times 3.14 \times 50 \times 15.0 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{F}\right)^{2}}{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{(200 \Omega)^{2}+(212.3 \Omega)^{2}}{/tex} = 291.67 {tex}\Omega{/tex} Therefore, the current in the circuit is {tex}I=\frac{V}{Z}=\frac{220 \mathrm{V}}{291.5 \Omega}=0.755 \mathrm{A}{/tex} Since the current is the same throughout the circuit, we have voltage across the resistor V R = IR = (0.75 A) (200 {tex}\Omega{/tex} ) = 151 V voltage across the capacitor V C = IX C = (0.755A) (212.3 {tex}\Omega{/tex} ) = 160.3 V The algebraic sum of the two voltages, V R and V C is 311.3 V which is more than the source voltage of 220 V. How to resolve this paradox? As you have learnt in the text, the two voltages are not in the same phase. Therefore, they cannot be added like ordinary numbers. The two voltages are out of phase by ninety degrees. Therefore, the total of these voltages must be obtained using the Pythagorean theorem: {tex}V_{R \cdot C}=\sqrt{V_{R}^{2}+V_{C}^{2}}{/tex} = 220 V Thus, if the phase difference between two voltages is properly taken into account, the total voltage across the resistor and the capacitor is equal to the voltage of the source.
solution_html:
Given
R = 200 {tex}\Omega{/tex} C = 15.0 {tex}\mu \mathrm{F}{/tex} = 15.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-6 F
V = 220 V,{tex}\nu{/tex}= 50 Hz

  1. In order to calculate the current, we need the impedance of the circuit. It is
    {tex}Z=\sqrt{R^{2}+X_{C}^{2}}=\sqrt{R^{2}+(2 \pi \nu C)^{2}}{/tex}
    {tex}=\sqrt{(200 \Omega)^{2}+\left(2 \times 3.14 \times 50 \times 15.0 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{F}\right)^{2}}{/tex}
    {tex}=\sqrt{(200 \Omega)^{2}+(212.3 \Omega)^{2}}{/tex}
    = 291.67 {tex}\Omega{/tex}
    Therefore, the current in the circuit is
    {tex}I=\frac{V}{Z}=\frac{220 \mathrm{V}}{291.5 \Omega}=0.755 \mathrm{A}{/tex}
  2. Since the current is the same throughout the circuit, we have
    voltage across the resistor  VR = IR = (0.75 A) (200 {tex}\Omega{/tex}) = 151 V
    voltage across the capacitor VC = IXC = (0.755A) (212.3 {tex}\Omega{/tex}) = 160.3 V
    The algebraic sum of the two voltages, VR and VC is 311.3 V which is more than the source voltage of 220 V. How to resolve this paradox? As you have learnt in the text, the two voltages are not in the same phase. Therefore, they cannot be added like ordinary numbers. The two voltages are out of phase by ninety degrees. Therefore, the total of these voltages must be obtained using the Pythagorean theorem:
    {tex}V_{R \cdot C}=\sqrt{V_{R}^{2}+V_{C}^{2}}{/tex}
    = 220 V
    Thus, if the phase difference between two voltages is properly taken into account, the total voltage across the resistor and the capacitor is equal to the voltage of the source.

subject: Physics
question_text: For circuits used for transporting electric power, a low power factor implies larger power loss in transmission. Explain. The power factor can often be improved by the use of a capacitor of appropriate capacitance in the circuit. Explain.
question_html:
1. For circuits used for transporting electric power, a low power factor implies larger power loss in transmission. Explain.
  2. The power factor can often be improved by the use of a capacitor of appropriate capacitance in the circuit. Explain.
solution_text: We know that P = E v I v cos {tex}\phi{/tex} where cos {tex}\phi{/tex} is the power factor. To supply/transmit a given power P at a given voltage E v ; if cos {tex}\phi{/tex} is small, l v has to be increased accordingly. therefore, power loss = {tex}I_{v}^{2}{/tex} R in transmission would increase. Power factor, cos {tex}\phi{/tex} = {tex}\frac{R}{Z}=\frac{R}{\sqrt{R^{2}+\left(X_{L}-X_{C}\right)^{2}}}{/tex} To improve power factor cos {tex}\phi{/tex} {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 1, for which Z {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} R. In a circuit with given value of L, we can use a capacitor of appropriate capacitance so that X C {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} X L .
solution_html:
1. We know that P = Ev Iv cos {tex}\phi{/tex} where cos {tex}\phi{/tex} is the power factor.
    To supply/transmit a given power P at a given voltage Ev ; if cos {tex}\phi{/tex} is small, lv has to be increased accordingly. therefore, power loss = {tex}I_{v}^{2}{/tex} R in transmission would increase.
  2. Power factor, cos {tex}\phi{/tex} = {tex}\frac{R}{Z}=\frac{R}{\sqrt{R^{2}+\left(X_{L}-X_{C}\right)^{2}}}{/tex}
    To improve power factor cos {tex}\phi{/tex} {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 1, for which Z {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} R. In a circuit with given value of L, we can use a capacitor of appropriate capacitance so that XC {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} XL.

subject: Physics
question_text: A sinusoidal voltage of peak value 283 V and frequency 50 Hz is applied to a series LCR circuit in which R = 3 {tex}\Omega{/tex} , L = 25.48 mH, and C = 796 {tex}\mu \mathrm{F}{/tex} . Find the impedance of the circuit; the phase difference between the voltage across the source and the current; the power dissipated in the circuit; and the power factor.
question_html:
A sinusoidal voltage of peak value 283 V and frequency 50 Hz is applied to a series LCR circuit in which R = 3 {tex}\Omega{/tex}, L = 25.48 mH, and C = 796 {tex}\mu \mathrm{F}{/tex}. Find

  1. the impedance of the circuit;
  2. the phase difference between the voltage across the source and the current;
  3. the power dissipated in the circuit; and
  4. the power factor.
solution_text: To find the impedance of the circuit, we first calculate X L and X C . X L = 2 {tex}\pi{/tex} vL = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 3.14 {tex}\times{/tex} 50 {tex}\times{/tex} 25.48 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 {tex}\Omega{/tex} = 8 {tex}\Omega{/tex} thus the impedence is given by;- {tex}X_{C}=\frac{1}{2 \pi v C}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{2 \times 3.14 \times 50 \times 796 \times 10^{-6}}=4 \Omega{/tex} Therefore, {tex}Z=\sqrt{R^{2}+\left(X_{L}-X_{C}\right)^{2}}{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{3^{2}+(8-4)^{2}}{/tex} = 5 {tex}\Omega{/tex} Phase difference, {tex}\phi=\tan ^{-1} \frac{X_{C}-X_{L}}{R}{/tex} = tan -1 {tex}\left(\frac{4-8}{3}\right){/tex} = -53.1° Since {tex}\phi{/tex} is negative, the current in the circuit lags the voltage across the source. The power dissipated in the circuit is given as:- P = I 2 R Now, {tex}I=\frac{i_{m}}{\sqrt{2}}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\left(\frac{283}{5}\right)=40 \mathrm{A}{/tex} Therefore, P {tex}=(40 \mathrm{A})^{2} \times 3 \Omega{/tex} = 4800 W Power factor = cos {tex}\phi{/tex} = cos (-53.1°) = 0.6
solution_html:
1. To find the impedance of the circuit, we first calculate XL and XC.
    XL= 2 {tex}\pi{/tex}vL
    = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 3.14 {tex}\times{/tex} 50 {tex}\times{/tex} 25.48 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 {tex}\Omega{/tex} = 8 {tex}\Omega{/tex} thus the impedence is given by;-
    {tex}X_{C}=\frac{1}{2 \pi v C}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{1}{2 \times 3.14 \times 50 \times 796 \times 10^{-6}}=4 \Omega{/tex}
    Therefore,
    {tex}Z=\sqrt{R^{2}+\left(X_{L}-X_{C}\right)^{2}}{/tex} {tex}=\sqrt{3^{2}+(8-4)^{2}}{/tex}
    = 5 {tex}\Omega{/tex}
  2. Phase difference, {tex}\phi=\tan ^{-1} \frac{X_{C}-X_{L}}{R}{/tex}
    = tan-1 {tex}\left(\frac{4-8}{3}\right){/tex} = -53.1°
    Since {tex}\phi{/tex} is negative, the current in the circuit lags the voltage across the source.
  3. The power dissipated in the circuit is given as:-
    P = I2R
    Now, {tex}I=\frac{i_{m}}{\sqrt{2}}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\left(\frac{283}{5}\right)=40 \mathrm{A}{/tex}
    Therefore, P {tex}=(40 \mathrm{A})^{2} \times 3 \Omega{/tex} = 4800 W
  4. Power factor = cos {tex}\phi{/tex} = cos (-53.1°) = 0.6

subject: Physics
question_text: Suppose the frequency of the source in the previous example can be varied. What is the frequency of the source at which resonance occurs? Calculate the impedance, the current, and the power dissipated at the resonant condition.
question_html:
Suppose the frequency of the source in the previous example can be varied.

  1. What is the frequency of the source at which resonance occurs?
  2. Calculate the impedance, the current, and the power dissipated at the resonant condition.
solution_text: The frequency at which the resonance occurs is {tex}\omega_{0}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{L C}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{25.48 \times 10^{-3} \times 796 \times 10^{-6}}}{/tex} = 222.1 rad/s {tex}\nu_{r}=\frac{\omega_{0}}{2 \pi}=\frac{221.1}{2 \times 3.14} \mathrm{Hz}{/tex} = 35.4 Hz The impedance Z at resonant condition is equal to the resistance: Z = R = 3 {tex}\Omega{/tex} The rms current at resonance is I:- {tex}=\frac{V}{Z}=\frac{V}{R}=\left(\frac{283}{\sqrt{2}}\right) \frac{1}{3}{/tex} = 66.7 A The power dissipated at resonance is given by:- P = I 2 {tex}\times{/tex} R = (66.7) 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 = 13.35 kW
solution_html:
1. The frequency at which the resonance occurs is
    {tex}\omega_{0}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{L C}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{25.48 \times 10^{-3} \times 796 \times 10^{-6}}}{/tex}
    = 222.1 rad/s
    {tex}\nu_{r}=\frac{\omega_{0}}{2 \pi}=\frac{221.1}{2 \times 3.14} \mathrm{Hz}{/tex} = 35.4 Hz
  2. The impedance Z at resonant condition is equal to the resistance:
    Z = R = 3 {tex}\Omega{/tex}
    The rms current at resonance is I:-
    {tex}=\frac{V}{Z}=\frac{V}{R}=\left(\frac{283}{\sqrt{2}}\right) \frac{1}{3}{/tex} = 66.7 A
    The power dissipated at resonance is given by:-
    P = I2 {tex}\times{/tex} R = (66.7)2 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 = 13.35 kW

subject: Physics
question_text: At an airport, a person is made to walk through the doorway of a metal detector, for security reasons. If she/he is carrying anything made of metal, the metal detector emits a sound. On what principle does this detector work?
question_html: At an airport, a person is made to walk through the doorway of a metal detector, for security reasons. If she/he is carrying anything made of metal, the metal detector emits a sound. On what principle does this detector work?
solution_text: The metal detector works on the principle of resonance in ac circuits. When you walk through a metal detector, you are, in fact, walking through a coil of many turns. The coil is connected to a capacitor tuned so that the circuit is in resonance. When you walk through with metal in your pocket, the impedance of the circuit changes - resulting in a significant change in current in the circuit. This change in current is detected and the electronic circuitry causes a sound to be emitted as an alarm and the person is caught having metal in the pocket.
solution_html: The metal detector works on the principle of resonance in ac circuits. When you walk through a metal detector, you are, in fact, walking through a coil of many turns. The coil is connected to a capacitor tuned so that the circuit is in resonance. When you walk through with metal in your pocket, the impedance of the circuit changes - resulting in a significant change in current in the circuit. This change in current is detected and the electronic circuitry causes a sound to be emitted as an alarm and the person is caught having metal in the pocket.

subject: Physics
question_text: A {tex}100\Omega{/tex} resistor is connected to a 220 V, 50 Hz ac supply. What is the rms value of current in the circuit? What is the net power consumed over a full cycle?
question_html:
A {tex}100\Omega{/tex} resistor is connected to a 220 V, 50 Hz ac supply.

  1. What is the rms value of current in the circuit?
  2. What is the net power consumed over a full cycle?
solution_text: Given, R = {tex}100\Omega{/tex} , V = 220V = V eff , f = 50 Hz We know, {tex}{I_{{\text{eff}}}} = \frac{{{V_{{\text{eff}}}}}}{R}{/tex} Putting values, we get, {tex}{I_{{\text{eff}}}} = \frac{{220}}{{100}}{/tex} = 2.2 A Power consumed = current {tex}\times{/tex} voltage = I eff {tex}\times{/tex} V eff = 2.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 220 = 484 watt
solution_html:
Given, R = {tex}100\Omega{/tex}, V = 220V = Veff, f = 50 Hz

  1. We know, {tex}{I_{{\text{eff}}}} = \frac{{{V_{{\text{eff}}}}}}{R}{/tex}
    Putting values, we get,
    {tex}{I_{{\text{eff}}}} = \frac{{220}}{{100}}{/tex} = 2.2 A
  2. Power consumed = current {tex}\times{/tex} voltage = Ieff {tex}\times{/tex}Veff
    = 2.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 220 = 484 watt

subject: Physics
question_text: The peak voltage of an ac supply is 300 V. What is the rms voltage? The rms value of current in an ac circuit is 10 A. What is the peak current?
question_html:
1. The peak voltage of an ac supply is 300 V. What is the rms voltage?
  2. The rms value of current in an ac circuit is 10 A. What is the peak current?
solution_text: In the given question, Peak voltage of the ac supply, V 0 is = 300 V rms voltage is given as: {tex}V=\frac{V_{0}}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{300}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} = 212.1 V The rms value of current is given as: I = 10 A Now, peak current is given as: {tex}I_{0}=\sqrt{2} I{/tex} {tex}=10 \sqrt{2}{/tex} = 14.1 A
solution_html:
1.  In the given question, Peak voltage of the ac supply, V0 is = 300 V
    rms voltage is given as:
    {tex}V=\frac{V_{0}}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{300}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} = 212.1 V
  2. The rms value of current is given as:
    I = 10 A
    Now, peak current is given as:
    {tex}I_{0}=\sqrt{2} I{/tex}
    {tex}=10 \sqrt{2}{/tex} = 14.1 A

subject: Physics
question_text: A 44 mH inductor is connected to 220 V, 50 Hz ac supply. Determine the rms value of the current in the circuit.
question_html: A 44 mH inductor is connected to 220 V, 50 Hz ac supply. Determine the rms value of the current in the circuit.
solution_text: Given L = 44 mH {tex} = 44 \times {10^{ - 3}}H{/tex} , f = 50 Hz, E rms = 220 V {tex}{X_L} = \omega L = 2\pi fL{/tex} {tex} = 2 \times 3.14 \times 50 \times44 \times {10^{ - 3}} {/tex} Now, {tex}{I_{rms}} = \frac{{{E_{rms}}}}{{{X_L}}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{220}}{{2 \times 3.14 \times 50 \times44 \times {10^{ - 3}} }}{/tex} = 15.9 A
solution_html:
Given L = 44 mH {tex} = 44 \times {10^{ - 3}}H{/tex}, f = 50 Hz, Erms = 220 V
{tex}{X_L} = \omega L = 2\pi fL{/tex}
{tex} = 2 \times 3.14 \times 50 \times44 \times {10^{ - 3}} {/tex}
Now, {tex}{I_{rms}} = \frac{{{E_{rms}}}}{{{X_L}}}{/tex}
{tex} = \frac{{220}}{{2 \times 3.14 \times 50 \times44 \times {10^{ - 3}} }}{/tex}
= 15.9 A

subject: Physics
question_text: A {tex}60\mu F{/tex} capacitor is connected to a 110 V, 60 Hz ac supply. Determine the rms value of the current in the circuit.
question_html: A {tex}60\mu F{/tex} capacitor is connected to a 110 V, 60 Hz ac supply. Determine the rms value of the current in the circuit.
solution_text: Given, {tex}C = 60\mu F = 60 \times {10^{ - 6}}F{/tex} E rms = 110V f = 60 Hz {tex}{I_{rms}} = \frac{{{E_{rms}}}}{{{X_C}}} = \frac{{{E_{rms}}}}{{\frac{1}{{C.\omega }}}} = {E_{rms}} \cdot C \cdot 2\pi f{/tex} Putting the values, {tex}{I_{rms}} = 110 \times 60 \times {10^{ - 6}} \times 2 \times 3.14 \times 60{/tex} = 2.49 A
solution_html:
Given, {tex}C = 60\mu F = 60 \times {10^{ - 6}}F{/tex}
Erms = 110V
f = 60 Hz
{tex}{I_{rms}} = \frac{{{E_{rms}}}}{{{X_C}}} = \frac{{{E_{rms}}}}{{\frac{1}{{C.\omega }}}} = {E_{rms}} \cdot C \cdot 2\pi f{/tex}
Putting the values,
{tex}{I_{rms}} = 110 \times 60 \times {10^{ - 6}} \times 2 \times 3.14 \times 60{/tex} = 2.49 A

subject: Physics
question_text: In Exercises 7.3 and 7.4, what is the net power absorbed by each circuit over a complete cycle? Explain your answer.
question_html: In Exercises 7.3 and 7.4, what is the net power absorbed by each circuit over a complete cycle? Explain your answer.
solution_text: In the inductive circuit, Rms value of current, I = 15.92 A Rms value of voltage, V = 220 V Hence, the net power absorbed can be obtained by the relation, P = VI cos {tex}\theta{/tex} where, {tex}\theta{/tex} = Phase difference between V and I For a pure inductive circuit, the phase difference between alternating voltage and current is 90° i.e., {tex}\theta{/tex} = 90° Hence, P = O i.e., the net power is zero. In the capacitive circuit, Rms value of current, I = 2.49 A Rms value of voltage, V = 110 V Hence, the net power absorbed can be obtained as: P = VI cos {tex}\theta{/tex} For a pure capacitive circuit, the phase difference between alternating voltage and current is 90° i.e., {tex}\theta{/tex} = 90° Hence, P = O i.e., the net power is zero.
solution_html:
In the inductive circuit,
Rms value of current, I = 15.92 A
Rms value of voltage, V = 220 V
Hence, the net power absorbed can be obtained by the relation,
P = VI cos {tex}\theta{/tex}
where,
{tex}\theta{/tex} = Phase difference between V and I
For a pure inductive circuit, the phase difference between alternating voltage and current
is 90° i.e., {tex}\theta{/tex} = 90°
Hence, P = O i.e., the net power is zero.
In the capacitive circuit,
Rms value of current, I = 2.49 A
Rms value of voltage, V = 110 V
Hence, the net power absorbed can be obtained as:
P = VI cos {tex}\theta{/tex}
For a pure capacitive circuit, the phase difference between alternating voltage and current is 90° i.e., {tex}\theta{/tex} = 90°
Hence, P = O i.e., the net power is zero.

subject: Physics
question_text: A charged 30 {tex}\mu F{/tex} capacitor is connected to a 27 mH inductor. What is the angular frequency of free oscillations of the circuit?
question_html: A charged 30 {tex}\mu F{/tex} capacitor is connected to a 27 mH inductor. What is the angular frequency of free oscillations of the circuit?
solution_text: In the given question, Capacitance, C = 30 {tex}\mu F{/tex} = 30 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -6 F Inductance, L = 27 mH = 27 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 H Angular frequency is given as: {tex}\omega_{r}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{L C}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{27 \times 10^{-3} \times 30 \times 10^{-6}}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{9 \times 10^{-4}}{/tex} = 1.11 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 3 rad/s Hence, the angular frequency of free oscillations of the circuit is = 1.11 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 3 rad/s
solution_html:
In the given question, Capacitance, C = 30 {tex}\mu F{/tex} = 30 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-6 F
Inductance, L = 27 mH = 27 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 H
Angular frequency is given as:
{tex}\omega_{r}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{L C}}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{27 \times 10^{-3} \times 30 \times 10^{-6}}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{9 \times 10^{-4}}{/tex} = 1.11 {tex}\times{/tex} 103 rad/s
Hence, the angular frequency of free oscillations of the circuit is = 1.11 {tex}\times{/tex} 103 rad/s

subject: Physics
question_text: A series LCR circuit with {tex}R = 20\Omega {/tex} L = 1.5 H and {tex}C = 35\mu F{/tex} is connected to a variable frequency 200 V ac supply. When the frequency of the supply equals the natural frequency of the circuit, what is the average power transferred to the circuit in one complete cycle?
question_html: A series LCR circuit with {tex}R = 20\Omega {/tex} L = 1.5 H and {tex}C = 35\mu F{/tex} is connected to a variable frequency 200 V ac supply. When the frequency of the supply equals the natural frequency of the circuit, what is the average power transferred to the circuit in one complete cycle?
solution_text: When the frequency of the a.c. supply is equal to the natural frequency, then {tex}Z=R{/tex} and {tex}\phi = {0^o}{/tex} {tex}\therefore Z=20 \Omega{/tex} {tex}I_v=\frac{E_v}{Z}=\frac{200}{20}{/tex} = 10 A Average power transferred, {tex}P=E_vI_v \cos 0^o{/tex} {tex}=200 \times 10 \times 1{/tex} = 2000 watt
solution_html:
When the frequency of the a.c. supply is equal to the natural frequency, then
{tex}Z=R{/tex} and {tex}\phi = {0^o}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore Z=20 \Omega{/tex}
{tex}I_v=\frac{E_v}{Z}=\frac{200}{20}{/tex} = 10 A
Average power transferred,
{tex}P=E_vI_v \cos 0^o{/tex}
{tex}=200 \times 10 \times 1{/tex} = 2000 watt

subject: Physics
question_text: A series of LCR circuit is connected to a variable frequency 230 V source, L = 5.0 H, {tex}C = 80\mu F{/tex} , {tex}R = 40\Omega {/tex} Determine the source frequency which drives the circuit in resonance. Obtain the impedance of the circuit and the amplitude of current at the resonating frequency. Determine the rms potential drops across the three elements of the circuit. Show that the potential drop across the LC combination is zero at the resonating frequency.
question_html:
A series of LCR circuit is connected to a variable frequency 230 V source, L = 5.0 H, {tex}C = 80\mu F{/tex}, {tex}R = 40\Omega {/tex}

  1. Determine the source frequency which drives the circuit in resonance.
  2. Obtain the impedance of the circuit and the amplitude of current at the resonating frequency.
  3. Determine the rms potential drops across the three elements of the circuit. Show that the potential drop across the LC combination is zero at the resonating frequency.
solution_text: Here, L = 5.0 H, {tex}R = 40\Omega {/tex} {tex}C = 80\mu F = 80 \times {10^{ - 6}}F{/tex} E v = 230 volt {tex}{E_0} = \sqrt 2 {E_v} = \sqrt 2 \times 230V{/tex} Resonance angular frequency, {tex}\omega_r = \frac{1}{{\sqrt {LC} }}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{1}{{\sqrt {5 \times 80 \times {{10}^{ - 6}}} }} = \frac{1}{{2 \times {{10}^{ - 7}}}}{/tex} = 50 rad/sec Impedance, {tex}Z = \sqrt {{R^2} + {{\left( {\omega L - \frac{1}{{\omega C}}} \right)}^2}} {/tex} At resonance, {tex}\omega L = \frac{1}{{\omega C}}{/tex} {tex}Z = \sqrt {{R^2}} = R = 40\Omega {/tex} Amplitude of current at resonating frequency, {tex}I_0=\frac{E_0}{Z}=\frac{\sqrt2 \times 230}{40} {/tex} = 8.13 A {tex}I_v=\frac{I_0}{\sqrt2}=\frac{8.13}{\sqrt2}=5.75{/tex} A Potential drop across L, {tex}{V_{L}}={I_v}{\omega _r}L = 5.75 \times 50 \times 5.0{/tex} = 1437.5 V Potential drop across R, {tex}{V_{R}}={I_v}\times R = 5.75 \times 40{/tex} = 230 V Potential drop across C, {tex}{V_{C}}={I_v}(\frac{1}{\omega_rC}){/tex} {tex}=5.75 \times \frac{1}{50 \times 80 \times 10 ^{-6}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{5.75}{4}\times 10^3{/tex} = 1437.5 V Potential drop across LC circuit, {tex}{V_{LC}}=​​V_{L}-V_{C}=0{/tex}
solution_html:
Here, L = 5.0 H, {tex}R = 40\Omega {/tex}
{tex}C = 80\mu F = 80 \times {10^{ - 6}}F{/tex}
Ev = 230 volt
{tex}{E_0} = \sqrt 2 {E_v} = \sqrt 2 \times 230V{/tex}

  1. Resonance angular frequency,
    {tex}\omega_r = \frac{1}{{\sqrt {LC} }}{/tex}
    {tex} = \frac{1}{{\sqrt {5 \times 80 \times {{10}^{ - 6}}} }} = \frac{1}{{2 \times {{10}^{ - 7}}}}{/tex} = 50 rad/sec
  2. Impedance, {tex}Z = \sqrt {{R^2} + {{\left( {\omega L - \frac{1}{{\omega C}}} \right)}^2}} {/tex}
    At resonance, {tex}\omega L = \frac{1}{{\omega C}}{/tex}
    {tex}Z = \sqrt {{R^2}} = R = 40\Omega {/tex}
    Amplitude of current at resonating frequency,
    {tex}I_0=\frac{E_0}{Z}=\frac{\sqrt2 \times 230}{40} {/tex} = 8.13 A
    {tex}I_v=\frac{I_0}{\sqrt2}=\frac{8.13}{\sqrt2}=5.75{/tex} A
  3. Potential drop across L,
    {tex}{V_{L}}={I_v}{\omega _r}L = 5.75 \times 50 \times 5.0{/tex} = 1437.5 V
    Potential drop across R,
    {tex}{V_{R}}={I_v}\times R = 5.75 \times 40{/tex} = 230 V
    Potential drop across C,
    {tex}{V_{C}}={I_v}(\frac{1}{\omega_rC}){/tex}
    {tex}=5.75 \times \frac{1}{50 \times 80 \times 10 ^{-6}}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{5.75}{4}\times 10^3{/tex} = 1437.5 V
    Potential drop across LC circuit,
    {tex}{V_{LC}}=​​V_{L}-V_{C}=0{/tex}
